source.definition: The parents of one's spouse; the mother and father of a son-in-law or daughter-in-law.

explanation.sourceText: The English term 'parents-in-law' is a compound phrase combining 'parents' with the legal term 'in-law', indicating a relationship by marriage rather than by blood.
explanation.targetText: Termenul englezesc „parents-in-law” este o expresie compusă care combină „parents” cu termenul legal „in-law”, indicând o relație prin căsătorie, nu prin sânge.

target.definition: Părinții soțului sau soției; părinții unui partener de căsătorie.

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian word 'socri' derives from the Latin word 'socrus', meaning 'mother-in-law', and was generalized in Romanian to refer to both parents-in-law.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul românesc „socri” provine din latinescul 'socrus', care însemna 'soacră', și a fost generalizat în română pentru a desemna ambii părinți ai soțului sau soției.

examples.sourceText: My parents-in-law are coming to visit tomorrow.
examples.targetText: Socrii mei vin mâine în vizită.

examples.sourceText: We have a good relationship with our parents-in-law.
examples.targetText: Avem o relație bună cu socrii noștri.

examples.sourceText: The parents-in-law often help with childcare.
examples.targetText: Socrii ajută adesea la îngrijirea copiilor.

learningTips.text: Remember 'socri' sounds like 'so' + 'cree'—think of your 'so' (significant other) and 'crew' (family team) to recall it means parents-in-law.
The Romanian 'socri' comes from Latin 'socrus' meaning mother-in-law, so it’s related to the English 'society'—both involve family connections.
Think of 'socri' as the 'social' parents connected by marriage, helping you link it to 'parents-in-law'.
Unlike the English phrase 'parents-in-law', Romanian uses a short, punchy word 'socri'—easy to remember and say!
